methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $10M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Chips & Semiconductors (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 14 same-sector peers exist in Chips & Semiconductors, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 20.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
Lab 91, a Seed-stage semiconductor chip company founded by Anand Chamarthy, has raised $10M+ to date. The valuation landscape for early-stage semiconductors remains sparse: while 14 private companies operate in the Chips & Semiconductors sector, peer-comparable data at identical stage and valuation tier is insufficient (fewer than 3 datapoints meeting narrow filters). The public comp universe comprises 3 major semiconductor players (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) with a median P/S multiple of 20.3x, though this cannot be applied without target revenue disclosure.

The analysis relies on Stage-Progression methodology, the only applicable approach given data constraints. Using the $10M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a standard 2.5x multiplier for Seed-to-next-stage progression yields an implied valuation of $25M. This reflects typical early-stage step-ups in the semiconductor subsector, where capital intensity and technical risk warrant elevated cross-stage multiples relative to software.

The triangulated range spans $21M (low) to $29M (high), with a median of $25M. This narrow band reflects dependence on a single method; robustness is limited. Key uncertainties include the target's proprietary technology differentiation, pathway to revenue, and capital requirements for tape-out or manufacturing partnerships—all material to semiconductor valuation but absent from available data. Peer-median anchoring remains impossible without either additional same-stage comps or disclosed revenue metrics.
